title: Your First Commit
intro: You've set up your Next.js project and you're ready to make your first commit. Think of it like sealing a time capsule — whatever you put in is preserved forever in your project's history. The habits you build now will follow you throughout the project. Let's see if you start clean.

teach.title: The First Commit Sets the Standard
teach.body: Your first commit should contain only the scaffolded project files with a clean .gitignore in place. No extra features, no experimental code, no leftover template content. This gives you a known-good baseline you can always return to if things go wrong.

teach.title: Sealing a Time Capsule
teach.body: Your first commit is like sealing a time capsule. Whatever you put in there is preserved forever in your project's history. You wouldn't toss your house keys into a time capsule — so don't commit your API keys either. Pack it carefully: only clean, intentional contents.

teach.title: The Pre-Flight Checklist
teach.body: Pilots don't just start the engine and take off — they run through a checklist every single time. Running `git status` before a commit is your pre-flight checklist. You're scanning for anything that shouldn't be on board: secret keys, bulky dependencies, system junk. Skipping the checklist might save you a minute, but one overlooked item can turn your flight into a disaster.

teach.title: Check Before You Commit
teach.body: Always run `git status` before committing to see exactly what you're about to save. Check for files that shouldn't be there — .env files with real secrets, node_modules, build output, or OS files like .DS_Store. A clean first commit means a clean project history.

This first commit is the foundation of a real product. Every successful app you see in the store started with exactly this moment — someone hitting 'commit' on a fresh project. You're now one of those people.
Before your first commit: verify .gitignore is correct, run git status to check what's staged, and write a clear commit message. This 60-second ritual prevents problems that are painful to fix later.

start.narrative: You just ran create-next-app and your task manager project is scaffolded. You want to make your first git commit. What do you do first?

choices.text: Run 'git status' to see what files are about to be committed
Run 'git add . && git commit -m initial commit' immediately
Skip the commit and start building features — you'll commit later when there's something worth saving

review-files.narrative: git status shows your project files. You notice that create-next-app already initialized git and created a .gitignore. You also see a .env.local file you created with your Supabase keys. What do you do?

choices.text: Open .gitignore to verify .env.local is listed, then check that no secrets appear in git status
The .gitignore is probably fine — just commit everything that shows up in git status

blind-commit.narrative: You committed everything without checking. The commit includes a .env.local file with your Supabase API key and a .DS_Store file from macOS. The API key is now in your git history. You realize the mistake before pushing anywhere. What's your move?

choices.text: Reset the local commit, add .env.local and .DS_Store to .gitignore, then start the commit flow over from scratch
It hasn't been pushed yet, so it doesn't matter — keep going

no-commit-recovery.narrative: After two hours of coding without a single commit, you accidentally delete a folder and lose work. You finally accept that the first commit has to happen now, before you write anything else. You're back at git status. How do you handle staging?

choices.text: Run git status, verify .gitignore covers .env files, and stage only the scaffolded files
git add . and hope for the best — at least it'll be saved

stage-files.narrative: You're ready to stage. git status lists about 30 scaffolded files. How do you stage them?

choices.text: git add . — you've already verified .gitignore so everything visible is intentional
git add app/ public/ package.json tsconfig.json next.config.js — explicit paths only
git add -p and accept every hunk one by one without reading them

compose-message.narrative: Files are staged. Time to write the commit message. What do you type?

choices.text: git commit -m 'Initialize Next.js project with TypeScript, Tailwind, and ESLint'

remote-setup.narrative: The commit lands on your local main branch. You want this work backed up off your laptop. What do you do next?

choices.text: Create a private repo on GitHub, add it as origin, and push the initial commit
Create a public repo on GitHub and push immediately so it's visible in your profile
Skip the remote — you can set it up later when there's more code worth backing up

excellent-end.narrative: Your first commit is clean: scaffold only, no secrets, descriptive message, and pushed to a private remote. You have a real backup, a clean history, and a baseline you can reset to. This is how a project should start.
excellent-end.feedback: Textbook first-commit hygiene. You verified status, respected .gitignore, staged intentionally, wrote a message future-you can search for, and got the work off your laptop. Every commit from here can follow this same discipline.

mediocre-end.narrative: The repo is backed up, but going public on day one means anyone can scan your code as you learn. If a secret slips into a future commit, it gets indexed by GitHub search before you notice.
mediocre-end.feedback: Public-by-default is fine for finished open source projects, but during the messy build phase, private repos give you room to fix mistakes quietly. Flip it to public when the app is launch-ready, not before.

weak-end.narrative: Your commit lives only on your laptop. A spilled coffee or a crashed disk wipes out the project, and there's nothing to clone from. The whole point of git was to have a recoverable history — and you skipped half of it.
weak-end.feedback: A local-only repo is half a backup. Push to a remote on day one — even if the project never goes public, the off-machine copy is what saves you from hardware failures, theft, and accidental rm -rf.
